metadata.description: Structured evaluation dataset for FOOTSIES reinforcement learning agent. 30 scripted scenarios across four tactical categories: whiff punishment (10), guard pressure response (8), special attack defense (6), and neutral/positional play (6).

format_notes.side: P1 means agent occupies the negative-X side of the stage; P2 means positive-X. 'forward' is resolved against agent.side at evaluation time.
format_notes.position: Continuous X coordinate. Agent always on the lower X if side=P1.
format_notes.guard: Integer 0-3. 0 means guard broken (one hit kills).
format_notes.move: Semantic name. Resolve to integer index via metadata.move_map. If your build of Footsies-Gym uses different indices, adjust the map only — scenarios stay valid.
format_notes.move_frame: Frame counter within the current move. Used to distinguish startup, active, and recovery phases.
format_notes.expected_action: Semantic action label. Resolve to MultiBinary(3) = [move_left, move_right, attack] via metadata.action_map, with FORWARD/BACKWARD direction resolved against agent.side.

move_map._comment: Best-guess indices based on the standard FOOTSIES FootsiesMove enum. Verify against your env's source (footsies_gym/moves.py or equivalent) and adjust if your fork differs.

action_map._comment: Each entry is [move_left, move_right, attack]. FORWARD and BACKWARD are agent-relative — resolve at evaluation by checking agent.side.

frame_phase_hints._comment: Approximate frame boundaries for the principal moves. Use these to validate that your scenario's move_frame falls in the intended phase.

scenarios.description: Opponent in mid-recovery of long attack at mid range.
scenarios.rationale: Opponent committed to a long attack and is now in recovery. Forward+attack closes the small remaining gap and lands during the recovery window before they can block.

scenarios.description: Opponent in normal-attack recovery at close range.
scenarios.rationale: Agent is already within normal-attack reach; the opponent's normal whiffed and is in recovery. A neutral attack lands without needing to advance and risks no overshoot.

scenarios.description: Opponent committed to long attack at far range — attack will whiff.
scenarios.rationale: Opponent is too far for their long attack to reach; they will enter recovery in open space. Forward (without attack) closes distance to set up the actual punish on the next decision step.

scenarios.description: Long attack deep recovery at the edge of normal-attack reach.
scenarios.rationale: Opponent is in deep recovery and within normal-attack range. Pure attack is preferred over forward+attack to avoid running past the hit-box.

scenarios.description: Long attack recovery far away — chase required.
scenarios.rationale: Opponent in recovery but distance prevents an immediate hit. Forward+attack reduces the gap while threatening; the attack input also reduces reaction window if opponent recovers earlier than expected.

scenarios.description: Opponent in committed forward dash at close range.
scenarios.rationale: Dashing extends the opponent's hurtbox in a predictable linear path with no defensive option. A direct attack catches them mid-dash before they can transition to attack or guard.

scenarios.description: Special-attack windup at mid range — interrupt window.
scenarios.rationale: Special is unblockable but has long windup. Forward+attack closes range and interrupts the windup before the active strike resolves, converting a defensive threat into a punish.

scenarios.description: Punish opportunity but agent guard is broken.
scenarios.rationale: Broken guard makes defense unviable on the next exchange anyway. Taking the certain punish on a recovering opponent is strictly better than retreating into another mixup.

scenarios.description: Cornered opponent in long-attack recovery.
scenarios.rationale: Opponent is recovering against the wall with no retreat option. Forward+attack secures the punish and preserves the corner position even on whiff.

scenarios.description: Normal attack just whiffed at close range.
scenarios.rationale: Opponent's normal attack is in early recovery. Even with the short recovery window of a normal, the agent's punish startup beats opponent's return-to-neutral at this range.

scenarios.description: Full guard, opponent active attack at close range.
scenarios.rationale: Holding backward blocks the active attack at the cost of one guard charge. With full guard available, accepting the trade is correct.

scenarios.description: Guard at 2, opponent attacking — block remains optimal.
scenarios.rationale: Guard at 2 still leaves a safety margin. Blocking maintains spacing and lets the agent react to follow-ups.

scenarios.description: Critical guard at 1, opponent attacking.
scenarios.rationale: Guard about to break, but the immediate threat is the active strike. Blocking now and gaining space lets guard regenerate before the next exchange.

scenarios.description: Broken guard, opponent advancing — escape.
scenarios.rationale: Any attack lands cleanly while guard is broken. Pure backward maximizes evasion distance and gives guard time to regenerate.

scenarios.description: Low guard but opponent in recovery — counter, do not retreat.
scenarios.rationale: Despite low guard, the opponent cannot defend during recovery. The punish window dominates the defensive risk; retreating wastes the opportunity.

scenarios.description: Full guard against close-range special windup.
scenarios.rationale: Special is unblockable; guard charges offer no protection. Normal attack during windup interrupts the special before its active frames resolve.

scenarios.description: Adequate guard, opponent walking — preempt their offense.
scenarios.rationale: Opponent is uncommitted (walking, not attacking). Advancing with attack claims tempo and forces a defensive reaction before they reach their preferred poking range.

scenarios.description: Cornered with low guard — must contest space outward.
scenarios.rationale: No retreat available against the wall. Staying passive guarantees pressure to guard break. Forward+attack contests opponent's advance and may force them to commit defensively.

scenarios.description: Special windup at close range — interrupt directly.
scenarios.rationale: Already within normal-attack reach; pure attack interrupts the windup. Forward input would overshoot.

scenarios.description: Special windup at mid range — close and interrupt.
scenarios.rationale: Windup phase still has ~18 frames remaining; forward step plus attack reaches in time to interrupt.

scenarios.description: Special windup at far range — evade.
scenarios.rationale: Distance is too great to reach the opponent before special becomes active. Backward dash maximizes the gap and forces a whiff.

scenarios.description: Cornered, opponent windup at mid range — must contest.
scenarios.rationale: Wall removes the backward evasion option. Forward+attack is the only winning play — contest the windup or take the hit.

scenarios.description: Late special windup at close range — last interrupt window.
scenarios.rationale: Only a few windup frames remain, but normal-attack startup is faster than the residual windup. Immediate attack still lands first at this range.

scenarios.description: Mid-range special windup with broken guard.
scenarios.rationale: Broken guard makes evading less safe (next exchange would be lethal anyway). Forward+attack attempts the interrupt — the higher-variance but only winning option.

scenarios.description: Symmetric neutral start — claim engagement initiative.
scenarios.rationale: Both characters at neutral. Forward (without attack) advances toward effective poking range without committing to a recovery-vulnerable move.

scenarios.description: Opponent cornered and idle — apply pressure.
scenarios.rationale: Cornered idle opponent has limited escape options. Forward+attack threatens the hit while denying any forward escape.

scenarios.description: Agent in corner, opponent at mid range and idle — escape carefully.
scenarios.rationale: Need to escape the corner before opponent commits. Pure forward (no attack) avoids whiffing into open air and preserves the option to react to opponent's next move.

scenarios.description: Mirror walk-forward — both committed at mid range.
scenarios.rationale: Both characters committed to forward movement and converging into poke range. Normal attack has the fastest startup; first to attack wins the exchange.

scenarios.description: Opponent retreating — chase aggressively.
scenarios.rationale: Opponent voluntarily yielding ground. Forward+attack contests the retreat and keeps them moving toward the wall without giving up tempo.

scenarios.description: Cornered against a special windup — only one winning play.
scenarios.rationale: Pinned in corner, unblockable threat incoming, no retreat. Forward+attack interrupts the windup — failure to commit guarantees loss.
